title: Dog Head Shaking and Whining: Causes and Triage Guide
meta_desc: Is your dog shaking their head and whining? Learn about potential causes, urgency levels, and how to help your pet find relief.
content_html:
What Is Your Dog Experiencing?

When a dog shakes their head repeatedly and whines, it is a clear sign of localized discomfort or pain, usually centered in the ear canal or the head area. This behavior is often an attempt to dislodge an irritant, such as a foreign object, or a reaction to the itching and burning sensation caused by an infection.

Urgency Level: Medium

This situation is classified as a medium urgency level. While it is rarely an immediate life-threatening emergency, your dog is likely in significant pain. If left untreated, vigorous shaking can cause an aural hematoma—a painful condition where blood vessels in the ear flap burst. You should aim to see a veterinarian within 24 to 48 hours to prevent further complications like eardrum damage.

Why a Photo or Video Helps

Capturing a quick video of the head-shaking behavior or a clear, well-lit photo of the inside of your dog's ear can be incredibly helpful for triage. A visual allows a professional to look for signs of inflammation, dark discharge, or visible obstructions. This information helps your vet determine if your pet needs to be squeezed in for an urgent fit-in appointment or if it can wait for a standard opening.

faqs.question: Could my dog have an ear infection?
faqs.answer: Yes, ear infections (bacterial or yeast) are the most common cause of head shaking and whining in dogs.

faqs.question: Should I try to clean my dog's ears myself?
faqs.answer: If your dog is whining in pain, do not attempt to clean the ears or insert any medication until a vet has confirmed the eardrum is intact.

faqs.question: What is an aural hematoma?
faqs.answer: It is a pocket of blood that forms inside the ear flap due to the trauma of excessive shaking, often requiring surgical drainage.
